curriculum.title: CSS Fundamentals
curriculum.description: Learn how to make webpages colorful, neat, and fun using CSS. CSS helps you style text, colors, spacing, and layouts.

lessons.title: What is CSS?
lessons.body: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets. CSS is used to decorate webpages by adding colors, changing text size, and arranging content neatly.
lessons.avatar_script: HTML builds the page, but CSS makes it beautiful! Think of CSS like clothes for your webpage. Without CSS, webpages look plain. With CSS, you can make them colorful, neat, and fun to read.

questions.question: CSS makes webpages look better.
questions.explanation: CSS adds style like colors and sizes.

questions.question: Change the paragraph text color to blue.
questions.explanation: Great job! The color property is one of the most commonly used CSS properties. It allows you to make your text stand out and match your webpage's design.

code_example.explanation: Let me show you how to change text color in CSS. First, I write the selector 'p' to target all paragraphs. Then I add curly brackets to hold my styles. Inside, I write 'color' which is the property that controls text color, followed by a colon. Then I write 'blue' as the value, and end with a semicolon. This tells the browser to make all paragraph text blue!

lessons.title: How CSS Talks to HTML
lessons.body: CSS works by choosing HTML elements and telling them how to look.
lessons.avatar_script: CSS talks to HTML by using names like p, h1, or body. These names tell CSS what to style. Then CSS gives instructions like color, size, or spacing.

questions.question: CSS can style headings and paragraphs.
questions.explanation: CSS can style many HTML tags.

questions.question: Make the h1 heading red.
questions.explanation: Excellent! You've learned how CSS selectors work. By using 'h1' as the selector, you can style all the main headings on your page at once!

code_example.explanation: Watch how I style a heading. I start with 'h1' - this is my selector that targets all h1 headings. Then I open curly brackets. Inside, I write 'color: red;' to make the heading text red. The colon separates the property from its value, and the semicolon ends the rule. Now all h1 headings will be red!

lessons.title: Writing Simple CSS Rules
lessons.body: A CSS rule has three parts: what to style, what to change, and the value.
lessons.avatar_script: A CSS rule is like a sentence. First, you say what you want to style. Then you say what you want to change. Finally, you give the value.

questions.question: What part chooses what to style?
CSS rules tell elements how to look.
Make paragraph text size 20px.
questions.explanation: Well done! The font-size property lets you control how big or small your text appears. Using pixels (px) gives you precise control over the exact size.

code_example.explanation: Here's how to change text size. I select paragraphs with 'p', then inside the curly brackets, I use the 'font-size' property. Notice it has a hyphen between 'font' and 'size'. I set it to '20px' - that's 20 pixels. Pixels are tiny dots on your screen, so 20px makes the text a nice readable size!

lessons.title: Text Colors
lessons.body: CSS can change the color of text to make it easy and fun to read.
lessons.avatar_script: Colors help people enjoy reading your page. CSS lets you change text colors using simple words like red, blue, or green.

questions.question: Change text color to green.
questions.explanation: Perfect! You're getting good at changing colors. CSS understands many color names like green, red, blue, purple, and more!

code_example.explanation: Let me show you how to make text green. I write 'p' to select paragraphs, then open my curly brackets. Inside, I write 'color: green;' - the property is 'color', and the value is 'green'. CSS knows many color names, so you can use words like green, blue, red, or even lightgreen and darkblue!

lessons.title: Background Colors
lessons.body: CSS can color the background of a webpage.
lessons.avatar_script: Background colors make your webpage stand out. You can color the whole page or just one part.

questions.question: Background color can be added to the body.
Make the page background lightblue.
questions.explanation: Awesome! Background colors make your webpage feel welcoming and fun. The body selector lets you style the entire page at once!

code_example.explanation: Watch how I color the whole page background. I use 'body' as my selector - this targets the entire webpage. Then I use 'background-color' as the property. Notice it has a hyphen! I set it to 'lightblue', which is a soft, pretty blue color. Now the whole page has a nice blue background!

lessons.title: Text Size
lessons.body: CSS can make text big or small.
lessons.avatar_script: Big text is easy to see, small text is neat. CSS lets you choose the size.

questions.question: Make the heading size 36px.
questions.explanation: Great work! Big headings grab attention and help readers know what your page is about. 36 pixels is a nice large size for main titles!

code_example.explanation: Let me make a big heading. I select 'h1' for the main heading, then use 'font-size' to control how big it is. I'm setting it to '36px' - that's 36 pixels, which makes a nice big title that's easy to see. Bigger numbers mean bigger text!

lessons.title: Padding
lessons.avatar_script: Padding is like space inside a box. It helps text breathe.

questions.question: Add 10px padding.
questions.explanation: Nice! Padding gives your content room to breathe. It creates space between your text and the edge of its container, making it easier to read.

code_example.explanation: Let me show you padding. Padding adds space INSIDE an element, like cushioning inside a box. I select 'p' for paragraphs, then write 'padding: 10px;'. This adds 10 pixels of space on all four sides - top, right, bottom, and left. Now the text won't touch the edges!

lessons.title: Margin
lessons.avatar_script: Margin is the space outside the box. It separates items.

questions.question: Add 15px margin.
questions.explanation: Well done! Margin creates space OUTSIDE elements, keeping them from bumping into each other. This makes your page look neat and organized.

code_example.explanation: Now let's learn about margin. Unlike padding which is inside, margin adds space OUTSIDE an element. Think of it like personal space between people! I write 'margin: 15px;' to add 15 pixels of space around the paragraph. This keeps it separated from other elements on the page.

lessons.title: Borders
lessons.avatar_script: Borders are outlines that help you see boxes clearly.

questions.question: Add a black border.
questions.explanation: Excellent! Borders help define the edges of elements and make them stand out. You can use different thicknesses, styles, and colors!

code_example.explanation: Let me show you how borders work. The border property needs three things: thickness, style, and color. I write 'border: 2px solid black;' - that's 2 pixels thick, a solid line (not dotted or dashed), and black color. All three values go together separated by spaces. Now the paragraph has a nice frame around it!

lessons.title: Styling Text Together
lessons.body: CSS can style text in many ways at once.
lessons.avatar_script: You can change color, size, and spacing together.

questions.question: CSS can use many rules together.
Make text brown and change size to 30px.
questions.explanation: Amazing! You've learned to combine multiple CSS properties together. This is how real web designers create beautiful, unique styles!

code_example.explanation: Now let's combine multiple styles! I select 'h2' for subheadings. Inside the curly brackets, I can add as many properties as I want. First, 'font-size: 30px;' makes it big. Then on a new line, 'color: brown;' makes it brown. Each property ends with a semicolon. You can keep adding more styles to make your text look exactly how you want!
